title: Dog Ate Grapes or Raisins: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate grapes or raisins, this is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn the risks of kidney failure and immediate steps to take.
content_html:
TL;DR: Grapes and raisins are highly toxic to dogs and can cause sudden kidney failure. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog ingests any amount, as even a single raisin can be fatal.

What is grape and raisin toxicity in dogs?

  • Grapes, raisins, and currants contain tartaric acid, which causes a sudden and severe toxic reaction in dogs.
  • Toxicity is unpredictable and not dose-dependent; a single fruit can be fatal to some dogs regardless of their weight.
  • Because individual sensitivity cannot be predicted, any ingestion must be treated with extreme caution.

Why is grape ingestion considered a high-urgency emergency?

  • Ingestion of grapes or raisins can lead to acute kidney failure within hours or days.
  • Signs of poisoning include vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, and loss of appetite.
  • Waiting for symptoms to appear often means the kidneys have already sustained significant damage.
  • You must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison helpline immediately, even if your dog is acting normally.

How does taking a photo of the fruit help the veterinary triage process?

  • A quick photo helps the vet confirm the exact type of fruit or packaging involved.
  • Triage teams use photos to check ingredient labels for co-toxins like xylitol or chocolate in trail mixes or baked goods.
  • Identifying additional toxins allows the veterinarian to initiate the correct treatment protocols more effectively.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Prompt decontamination is recommended following significant grape or raisin ingestion. Emesis may be induced using 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, not exceeding 45 mL), followed by activated charcoal. Aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended for large ingestions or cases with spontaneous vomiting or diarrhea within 12 hours of ingestion. Renal function and fluid balance should be closely monitored during fluid administration. For oliguric patients, urine production may be stimulated with dopamine (0.5-3 mcg/kg/min, IV) and/or furosemide (2 mg/kg, IV). Anuric patients are unlikely to survive without peritoneal or hemodialysis, and even then, the prognosis is guarded. Affected dogs typically develop vomiting and/or diarrhea within 6-12 hours of ingestion, along with lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared to serum urea nitrogen levels. Oliguric or anuric renal failure can develop within 24-72 hours of exposure.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How many grapes are toxic to dogs?
faqs.answer: There is no known safe dose for grapes or raisins. Because some dogs are significantly more sensitive than others, any amount consumed—even one—is considered a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of grape poisoning?
faqs.answer: The most common early sign is vomiting, usually within 6 to 12 hours of ingestion. Other signs include lethargy, dehydration, abdominal pain, and a change in urination frequency.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. Doing so incorrectly can lead to aspiration pneumonia or other complications.
